full_response:
Let  

 N = 2n (the total number of seats), n = N/2 (the number of ordinary guests).

After the n guests have finished seating, every seat is either  

 occupied – call the seat black – or  
 empty – call the seat white.

A block is a maximal sequence of consecutive black seats whose two boundary seats are white.
If seat 0 is black, Sophie will walk clockwise across exactly the first block that starts at
seat 0 and will therefore stop exactly |block| seats away from her favourite seat.  
Hence

 Sophie’s distance from seat 0  ≤  length of the longest block.             (1)

Thus it suffices to prove

 with probability ≥ 1 – 1/N² the longest block has length O(log n).         (2)

The remainder of the solution is devoted to (2).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1 How can a long block arise?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Fix an interval I of L consecutive seats.  Write H(I) for the number of random
hashes r1,…,rn that fall into I.

Lemma 1  
If I is a block in the final configuration then H(I) ≥ L.

Proof.  
Let seats of I be numbered s, … , s+L–1 (mod N) and let s–1 be the white seat
immediately preceding the block.
Take any seat t∈I with final occupant g.
During the insertion of g the seats s,…,t–1 were already black, whereas s–1
was (and still is) white.  
If g had hashed to a position < s, her walk would have reached the still empty seat
s–1 and she would have sat there, contradiction.
Therefore g’s hash r_g lies in I.  Since the argument works for every one of the
L occupants of I, we must have H(I) ≥ L. ∎



--------------------------------------------------------------------
2 How many hashes can hit a fixed interval?
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For any fixed interval I of length L the random variable H(I) is

 H(I) ~  Binomial( n , p = L/N ).

Hence E[H(I)] = n·L/N = (N/2)·L/N = L/2.

We want H(I) ≥ L, i.e. at least twice its expectation.  
A Chernoff bound (δ = 1) gives

 Pr[ H(I) ≥ L ] = Pr[ H(I) ≥ (1+δ)E ] ≤ (e¹ / 2²)^{E} = (e/4)^{L/2}.         (3)

Because e/4 ≈ 0.68, (3) decays exponentially with L.



--------------------------------------------------------------------
3 No long block anywhere with high probability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let L₀ = C log n for a constant C that will be chosen shortly.
Use the union bound over

 • every starting position (N possibilities) and  
 • every length L ≥ L₀.

Summing the geometric series produced by (3):

Pr[∃ block of length ≥ L₀]  
 ≤ ∑_{L=L₀}^{∞} N·(e/4)^{L/2}  
 ≤ N·(e/4)^{L₀/2} · 1 / (1 – √{e/4})                (because e/4<1)  
 ≤ 6N·(e/4)^{L₀/2}.                                        (4)

Choose C large enough so that the right–hand side of (4) is ≤ 1/N².
Because ln((e/4)^{1/2}) = ½ ln(e/4) = –0.194…, taking

 C ≥ 20                                                       (5)

is more than sufficient: with (5) the exponent in (4) is at most –3 ln n, and

 6N·n^{–3} = 12n·n^{–3} = 12/n² ≤ 1/N²           for every n ≥ 12.

Therefore

 Pr[ longest block length ≥ 20 log n ] ≤ 1/N².             (6)



--------------------------------------------------------------------
4 Finishing the argument for Sophie
--------------------------------------------------------------------
By (6) we have  

 with probability at least 1 – 1/N² every block has length < 20 log n.

In that event, if seat 0 is white Sophie sits there;  
if it is black she passes exactly one block whose length is < 20 log n.
Thus from (1)

 Pr[ Sophie’s seat number > 20 log n ] ≤ 1/N².

Hence Sophie sits on a seat numbered O(log n) clockwise from 0
with probability at least 1 – 1/N², completing the proof. ∎



\boxed{\text{With probability }\ge 1-\frac1{N^{2}},\ \text{Sophie’s seat number is at most }C\log n\ (\text{for a suitable constant }C).}

Confidence: 68%
